title: Git Internals
learningObjectives: Explain the 4 Git object types: blob, tree, commit, and tag
Inspect Git objects with git cat-file plumbing commands
Understand refs, HEAD, and the packed-refs file
Explain the reftable backend introduced as production-ready in Git 2.47+
Use plumbing commands to walk the Git object graph
Understand packfiles and how delta compression reduces storage

theory.content:
Git is a Content-Addressable Filesystem

At its core, Git is a content-addressable filesystem — every piece of data stored in Git is identified by the SHA-1 hash of its content. Change a single byte and you get a completely different SHA. This makes Git inherently tamper-evident and allows deduplication: identical files across commits share the exact same blob object.

There are exactly four object types in the Git object model:

  • blob — raw file content (no filename, no metadata)
  • tree — a directory listing: ordered entries of mode, type, name, and SHA pointing to blobs or sub-trees
  • commit — a snapshot: points to a root tree object, zero or more parent commits, and carries author, committer, timestamp, and message
  • tag — an annotated tag object pointing to any other object with tagger metadata and a PGP signature slot

Objects are stored in .git/objects/ as zlib-compressed files. The first 2 hex characters of the SHA form the directory name; the remaining 38 characters form the filename. For example, SHA a1b2c3d4e5f6... lives at .git/objects/a1/b2c3d4e5f6....

Object Types in Detail

ObjectContainsCreated by
blobRaw file content — no filename or path informationgit add
treeList of blobs and sub-trees with octal file mode and entry namegit commit
commitRoot tree SHA + parent SHA(s) + author/committer/date + messagegit commit
tagPoints to any object + tagger name/email/date + tag messagegit tag -a

Because blobs contain only file content, renaming a file does not create a new blob — the same blob is simply referenced under a different name in the tree object. This is how Git tracks renames efficiently.

Plumbing Commands

Plumbing commands are Git's low-level building blocks, as opposed to porcelain commands (like git commit, git merge) which are designed for human use. Plumbing commands are stable, scriptable, and reveal the internals:

# Inspect object type by SHA
git cat-file -t SHA

# Pretty-print object content by SHA
git cat-file -p SHA

# Write a file to the object store and print its SHA
git hash-object -w filename

# List the root tree of HEAD
git ls-tree HEAD

# List recursively (all files)
git ls-tree -r HEAD

# Resolve a ref or expression to a raw SHA
git rev-parse HEAD

# 3 commits back from HEAD
git rev-parse HEAD~3

# Show the SHA of a specific branch
git rev-parse refs/heads/main

Combining these lets you walk the entire object graph manually: resolve HEAD to a commit SHA, pretty-print the commit to find its tree SHA, list that tree to find blob SHAs, then cat-file those blobs to read raw file content.

Refs and HEAD

A ref is a named pointer to a SHA-1 object hash. Refs are how human-readable names (branch names, tag names) map to commit SHAs. They are stored as plain text files under .git/refs/:

  • .git/refs/heads/ — local branches
  • .git/refs/tags/ — tags
  • .git/refs/remotes/ — remote-tracking branches

HEAD lives at .git/HEAD. Normally it is a symbolic ref: the file contains the text ref: refs/heads/main, meaning HEAD points to whatever commit the main branch points to. When you git checkout SHA directly, HEAD becomes a raw SHA — this is called detached HEAD state.

Packed-refs: after git gc or when the repo has many refs, Git consolidates loose ref files into a single .git/packed-refs file for performance. Loose refs still take precedence over packed-refs. The git refs command (stable since Git 2.47) provides a safe interface: git refs list and git refs delete refs/heads/old-branch.

Reftable Backend

The traditional ref storage (loose files + packed-refs) has limitations: linear scan for packed-refs lookup, race conditions with concurrent writers, and no transaction log. The reftable backend was introduced as an experiment in Git 2.45 and became production-ready in Git 2.47+.

Reftable stores refs in a sorted binary table of records, replacing both .git/refs/ loose files and .git/packed-refs. Key benefits:

  • O(log n) lookups via binary search vs O(n) sequential scan for packed-refs
  • Atomic updates — all ref changes in a single operation are atomic, preventing partial-update corruption
  • Transaction log — built-in reflog stored alongside ref records
  • Safe for concurrent access — lock-free read paths and writer serialization

To use reftable on a new repo: git init --ref-format=reftable. On an existing repo, set core.repositoryformatversion = 1 and add extensions.refStorage = reftable in .git/config. Large hosting providers (GitHub, GitLab) use reftable internally for massive repo scalability.

Packfiles

Loose objects (one file per SHA) are efficient for writes but wasteful for storage and slow for network transfer. Git periodically runs git gc (garbage collection) or git repack to pack loose objects into packfiles using delta compression.

A packfile consists of two files: .pack (compressed object data) and .idx (index file for O(log n) lookup of any object by SHA without scanning the entire pack). Delta compression stores objects as a base object plus a binary diff, dramatically reducing storage for similar files across commits.

# Count loose objects and packfile stats
git count-objects -v

# Trigger garbage collection and repacking
git gc

# List all objects in a packfile with their sizes
git verify-pack -v .git/objects/pack/*.idx

# Aggressive repack (more CPU, better compression)
git repack -a -d -f --depth=250 --window=250
